Tafseer:
طسم (١)
In our view, "Ta Seen Meem" is the predicate (Khabar) of an omitted subject (Mubtada). We have disclosed this omitted element in the translation, meaning: 'This is Surah Ta Seen Meem.'

تلک ایت الکتب المبین (٢)
The sun itself is the proof of the sun!
'The clear Book' obviously refers to the Holy Qur'an, which has clearly substantiated and proven every aspect of its message in a highly eloquent and articulate language through diverse styles. The purpose of mentioning this attribute of the Qur'an here is to console the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that the Book he is presenting to the people is itself the greatest proof of its own authenticity and truth. It does not require any external evidence. Therefore, the demand of those who seek a miracle or a sign of punishment to believe in it is not worthy of attention, and you should not worry about it. The sun is its own proof; no external evidence is established to prove it.

Heart-soothing consolation
The theme of consolation that was implicit in the preceding verse is made explicitly clear in a very affectionate manner in this verse. Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it is said that their refusal to believe in this clear Book weighs so heavily on your heart that it seems you might destroy yourself out of grief. However, the fault lies neither with you nor with this Book; the entire blame lies with these people themselves who are seeking various excuses to deny an absolutely obvious reality. Your responsibility is only to convey the message. You have fully discharged this duty and continue to do so; why then should you exhaust yourself over such ungrateful people?

A stylistic feature of Arabic
Apparently, the word 'Khadiah' (feminine) should have been used instead of 'Khadieen' (masculine plural) to agree with 'Anaq' (necks). However, 'Khadieen' is used here in consideration of the possessive pronoun (Mudaf Ilayh) in 'Anaquhum' (their necks). This is a well-known style in the Arabic language, and some examples of this have appeared previously.

Consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and a warning for the opponents
In this verse, along with consolation for the Holy Prophet, there is also an implicit threat to the disbelievers. It means that if these people are adamant about seeing a sign before they believe, they should remember that We do not lack signs. Whenever We wish, We can send down a sign from heaven before which all their necks will bow down. However, We want them to believe out of their own free will and understanding. In Our sight, the credible faith is the one embraced out of free choice and intention, not under compulsion. This provides consolation to the Prophet that since this is the divine sunnah regarding faith, you should not be distressed by their demands; leave their matter to God!

Verses 5-6
The aversion of the sick and their ultimate end
This carries the same meaning as the final verse of Surah al-Furqan, though expressed in slightly different words. It means that the entire arrangement of reminding and teaching made by the Merciful Lord was solely intended to make these people think, reflect, and adopt the correct path of life. However, their condition is such that the more treatment was attempted, the more their aversion to the medicine and the physician grew. Allah reminded them through fresh and diverse styles, yet they persisted in turning away and mocked both the Qur'an and the Messenger. Following this attitude of theirs, nothing remains for them now except that the consequences warned of by the Qur'an—which they have mocked so far—manifest before them one by one, and indeed they shall face them.

The word 'Zawj' means kind or category. 'Min kulli zawjin' means diverse kinds of things. Here, 'Kareem' is used in the sense of beneficial and advantageous; the Arabs call grapes 'Karam' due to their highly beneficial nature.

Indication towards the signs on earth
Why do they not look at these diverse blessings spread across the earth, which the Merciful and Noble Lord has created for the purpose that people may benefit from them and derive insight and reminders?

Verses 8-9
If they are indeed demanding a sign, what greater sign could there be than this! Here, keep in mind all the arguments for monotheism, the Resurrection, and recompense that are mentioned in detail throughout the Qur'an, and in support of which the Qur'an refers to these blessings and bounties of the earth. This subject will be fully elaborated later under verse 10 of Surah Luqman and verse 27 of Surah as-Sajdah.

'And most of them were not believers' (Wa ma kana aktharuhum mu'minin) means that it is not due to a lack of signs; indeed, every corner of this earth is filled with signs, but what cure is there for those who do not wish to believe!

The practice of respite by the All-Mighty, All-Merciful God
'And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful' (Wa inna Rabbaka la huwal-Azizur-Rahim). Allah has clarified the aforementioned point in the light of His attributes: your Lord is both Mighty (Aziz) and Merciful (Rahim). Mighty means Dominant, He who can do whatever He wills, and none can stay His hand. If He were to send down an immediate punishment upon them, none could save them from His grasp. Yet, He is also Merciful, which is why He does not hasten the punishment, so that those who wish to repent and reform may utilize this respite to do so and deserve His mercy. The repetition of the subject here is specifically intended to emphasize these attributes and draw attention to them, because failing to keep these attributes in mind led the ignorant to mistake God's respite as a proof of the correctness of their behavior, causing them to arrogantly mock those who invited them to reform. These verses will recur frequently in this Surah, so commit their meaning well to heart so that there will be no need to repeat this explanation later.

The upcoming subject... Verses 10-68
The story of Prophet Moses and Pharaoh
Next, the story of Moses (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh, which was briefly alluded to in the previous Surah, is presented in detail. This narrative begins from the time Moses and Aaron were commanded to go to Pharaoh and concludes with the drowning of Pharaoh and his army. It points to all the major events of that period. While this portion of the narrative serves as a source of comfort and consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his Companions, and a lesson for his opponents, the specific point of focus reflecting the central theme of the Surah is that those who become slaves to personal desires and lose their faculty of reason are not affected by even the greatest miracles. Their eyes open only when their ultimate doom is laid out right before them. Read the verses in the light of this.

Verses 10-12
This refers to the event in the story of Moses when, on his return from Madyan, Allah blessed him with His direct discourse in the sacred valley of Tuwa, elevated him to the station of messengership, and directed him to go to Pharaoh and his people to warn them.

A specific style of expression
The phrasing 'Will they not fear?' (Ala yattaqun) highlights the extreme transgression and corruption of Pharaoh and his people. Its true sense is: Will these oppressors continue to plunge blindly into their rebellion and not fear its consequences and the wrath of God? It will be seen later from verses 125, 143, 162, and 178 that other noble prophets warned their respective nations using this exact expression. The emotions of sorrow, anger, rebuke, and the warning of the proximity of divine punishment inherent in this form of address require no further elaboration.

Verses 13-14
The apprehensions of Moses upon receiving prophethood
Moses was greatly humbled and frightened by this heavy responsibility, and such fear is a natural consequence of a true sense of duty. Even before leaving for Madyan, he was well aware of how difficult it was to speak the truth before the Pharaoh's court. Furthermore, he felt a lack of eloquence in his speech and was apprehensive due to the incident involving the death of the Coptic man at his hands. He knew that Pharaoh and his nobles were harboring anger against him and would not easily forgive him, especially when he would present his claim of messengership and warn them of their tyranny and injustice. Along with expressing these apprehensions, Moses requested that if it was the divine will for him to carry this heavy burden, his brother Aaron, who was highly eloquent in speech, should be appointed as his deputy and associate so they could perform this service effectively together.

Verses 15-17
Reassurance from Allah
Allah firmly dispelled the apprehension of Moses that the Copts would kill them, but approved his request regarding Aaron—as explicitly stated in other places and alluded to here—commanding both of them to go to Pharaoh without fear. 'Indeed, We are with you, listening' (Inna ma'akum mustami'un) is a highly eloquent phrase of reassurance. In another chapter, this is stated as: 'Indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see' (Ta-Ha: 46), meaning: how could anyone dare to cause you harm?

Two messengers instead of one to Pharaoh
From the words 'Indeed, we are messengers of the Lord of the worlds' (Inna Rasulu Rabbil-Alamin), it is understood that Aaron was an assistant and a partner in messengership. This is further clarified in another place: 'Indeed, we are messengers of your Lord' (Ta-Ha: 47). Declaring this fact to Pharaoh served to establish complete proof, showing that Allah had sent not one but two messengers to him; if he still refused to mend his ways, his ruin would be entirely his own doing.

Maulana Farahi's perspective on 'Send with us the Children of Israel'
The explanation of 'Send with us the Children of Israel' (An arsil ma'ana Bani Isra'il) has been detailed in the commentary of Surah Ta-Ha.
While it is known from the Torah that Moses wanted to take the Children of Israel a three-day journey into the wilderness to observe a sacrificial festival, the Torah does not specify where in the wilderness he intended to take them. The Ustadh-e-Imam (may Allah have mercy on him) was of the view that Moses wanted to take them to Makkah for pilgrimage and sacrifice. I have presented this view of the Maulana as a theory, but since his complete arguments are not before me, I am not fully certain about it. I have expressed my leaning in the commentary of Surah al-A'raf. Some points do support the Maulana's view, and I may clarify them at an appropriate place later. Along with warning Pharaoh, this was the first instruction to Moses regarding the organization of the Children of Israel and the revival of faith among them.

Reason for the ambiguity in the Torah regarding this event
There are two possible reasons for this ambiguity in the Torah's narrative: either Moses chose to keep the destination vague to hide it from Pharaoh, or the Jewish scribes, finding its disclosure contrary to their interests, concealed this fact just as they drew a veil over the history of Ishmael and the Kaaba.

'An arsil' is originally 'Bi-an arsil'. According to the standard rules of Arabic grammar, the preposition 'bi' has been omitted. The meaning is: 'We have come as messengers with this message.'

Verses 18-19
Pharaoh's contemptuous response to Moses
Based on contextual clues, some details are omitted here: that Moses, in compliance with Allah's command, went with his brother Aaron to Pharaoh and his courtiers, warning them and demanding that the Children of Israel be allowed to leave with him. How could Pharaoh ever imagine that anyone would stand before him with such a grand claim and such a massive demand—and that too, an Israelite! He said in utter contempt: 'Did we not raise you among us as a child, and did you not commit that deed of yours which you did? Indeed, you are of the ungrateful!' Here, 'kaafir' is used in its literal sense, meaning ungrateful, and 'you did that deed of yours' was a reference to the killing of the Coptic man, utilizing an elliptical style to emphasize its gravity. His message was: 'Know your place! Despite being raised by us and being a criminal in our eyes, you stand before us claiming prophethood and demanding to take the Children of Israel!'

Verses 20-21
Here, 'dall' is used in the same sense as in 'And He found you lost and guided you' (Ad-Duha: 7), meaning a seeker wandering in search of the path, or one who is lost.

Moses' confession of his mistake and declaration of truth
Regarding the incident of the Coptic man, Moses openly admitted his mistake, saying: 'Indeed, I committed that act, but I did so at a time when I had not yet received the true light of knowledge from my Lord; rather, I was still wandering in search of knowledge. When that act occurred and I feared you would kill me for this unintentional mistake, I fled from here. After that, my Lord bestowed upon me judgment and wisdom, gave me the power to distinguish between truth and falsehood, and made me one of His messengers. Now, I have come to you as a messenger. My current status is entirely different from my past state; disregard that past event and focus on what I am saying.'

We have discussed the nature of the Coptic man's killing in detail in Surah Ta-Ha, explaining that it occurred entirely without intent while Moses was trying to assist an oppressed person. Moses deeply regretted it, repented, and Allah accepted his repentance.

Intellect alone is not sufficient for guidance
We have also discussed under Surah Hud (verse 17) that the prophets possessed a sound nature (fitrah) even before their formal commission. However, nature and intellect alone are not sufficient to guide a human being through the intricate paths of life. There are many turning points where human intellect is left bewildered, and it cannot make the correct decision without divine guidance. It is this wisdom and recognition that Moses has referred to here as 'judgment' (Hukm). Further explanation will follow under verse 83.

Moses' eloquent response
Moses gave a highly eloquent response to Pharaoh's reminder of his past favor mentioned in verses 18-19. He said: 'Are you bringing up this favor of yours to justify your grave injustice of enslaving the Children of Israel?' The meaning is: 'Granted, you did me a favor, and I thank you for it! But this favor does not give you the right to keep the entire nation of the Children of Israel in slavery, nor does it mean I should remain silent against it!'

Mockery of the claim of messengership
Pharaoh was so silenced by the response to his taunt and reminder of favors that he had nothing left to say on that front. Therefore, to maintain his prestige, he tried to mock Moses' claim of messengership. It was mentioned in verse 16 that Moses and Aaron had presented themselves as messengers of the Lord of the worlds. Pharaoh mocked this, asking: 'And what is the Lord of the worlds?' His implication was: 'I am the supreme deity and the lord of this land; who else is this "Lord of the worlds" who has sent you as messengers?'

قال رب السموت والارض وما بینھا ان کنتم موقنین (24)
Taking the call a step further
In response to Pharaoh's insolent sarcasm, Moses advanced his invitation one step further, saying: 'Your Lord of the worlds is the One who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them.' It should be noted here that 'all that is between them' includes the sun and the moon, and Pharaoh—as explained elsewhere—was considered the incarnation of the sun god.

'If you should be convinced' (In kuntum muqineen) means: 'This is the actual reality I have made clear. Whether you accept it or not, the responsibility lies on you, not on me.'

قال لمن حولہ الا تستمعن (25)
Pharaoh's bewilderment
Moses' response was a severe blow to Pharaoh's pride, causing him to lose his composure. He turned to his courtiers and said: 'Are you listening to what this man is saying?'

قال ربکم و رب ابآئکم الاولین (26)
Advancing the call yet another step
Without paying any heed to Pharaoh's anger, Moses advanced his message further: 'Your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers!' It is worth noting here that false religions are built entirely on the blind following of ancestors. Therefore, Moses immediately clarified this reality, implying: 'If you are relying on the traditions of your forefathers in this matter, then this reliance is baseless. Their Lord was also the One whom I am calling you to. If they left Him to worship others, it was their own deviation and ignorance. Do not destroy your afterlife by blindly following them.'

قال ان رسولکم الذی ارسل الیکم لمجنون (27)
Pharaoh's frustration
When Moses exposed the deviation of their ancestors, Pharaoh's anger peaked. Out of frustration, he said to his courtiers in a highly condescending and sarcastic tone: 'This messenger of yours, who claims to have been sent to you, must be completely mad!' His implication was: 'Since this man is calling our forefathers foolish and deviated, there is no doubt that he is insane!' His phrasing 'This messenger of yours who was sent to you' also carried an attempt to instigate the people, as if to say: 'Look at this man who claims to be a messenger sent to you!'

The final blow to Pharaoh's claim of divinity
Moses paid no attention to Pharaoh's insolence, completely ignored it, and advanced his call yet another step. He said: 'The Lord of the east and the west and all that is between them, if you would only use your common sense!' This was Moses' final blow to Pharaoh's claim of divinity. Pharaoh considered himself the incarnation of the sun. Moses made it clear that a person who has no control over either the east or the west and yet claims to be lord is suffering from a defect of intellect, as are those who accept him. If you use your reason, this matter is so obvious that there is no reason for anyone to find it difficult to understand.

قال لین اتخذت الھا غیری لاجعلنک من المسجونین 29
Pharaoh's threat
When Pharaoh saw that Moses did not give any weight to his words, but fearlessly moved from one argument to another—each more powerful than the last and dealing a direct blow to his claim of divinity—he lost all patience and shouted: 'If you take a god other than me, I will surely place you among the prisoners!'

قال اولو جئتک بشیء مبین ( 30)
Offering a clear sign
Pharaoh likely expected that Moses would be intimidated by this open threat. Instead, Moses responded with great composure and dignity: 'Even if I bring you a clear, undeniable sign of my truthfulness?'

قال فات بہ ان کنت من الصدقین (31)
Moses showed the miracles upon Pharaoh's demand after presenting the call
Pharaoh said: 'Bring it then, if you are of the truthful.'

فالتی عصاہ فاذا ہی ثعبان مبین، ونزع یدہ فاذا ھی بیضآء للنظرین (33-32)
Upon this demand, Moses showed both miracles: the staff and the shining white hand. The explanation of these miracles has already been given in Surah Ta-Ha. Here, it is particularly noteworthy that Moses displayed these miracles only after Pharaoh threatened to imprison him in response to his call of monotheism, which was fully based on intellect, nature, and the signs of the universe.

Verses 34-35
The overwhelming nature of the miracles and Pharaoh's cleverness
The overwhelming nature of these miracles was so apparent that Pharaoh and his courtiers were left stunned. However, Pharaoh cleverly tried to prevent the people from being influenced. He acknowledged the performance of Moses, as it was undeniable, but immediately labeled it as a mere display of sorcery, asserting that it had nothing to do with prophethood or messengership.

Furthermore, to stir political suspicion against Moses, he sowed the seed of doubt in the minds of the people, saying: 'This man wants to win over the public through his magic and expel us from our land.' We have explained elsewhere that the prevailing atmosphere of that time made it very easy to spread such suspicions. Using this fear, Pharaoh asked the courtiers: 'What do you advise to counter this threat?'

37-36
The courtiers' advice
The courtiers advised that it would be best to counter magic with magic, but cautioned that a contest against ordinary magicians might be risky, as defeat would bring public humiliation. Therefore, they suggested putting Moses off for a time while sending state emissaries to all cities to gather the most expert magicians of the land for a grand contest.

Verses 38-40
Preparations for the contest against Moses
The courtiers' advice was put into action. Arrangements were made to gather the most expert magicians of the country at the center on a specific day and at a designated time. It is specified in other places that the day of a public festival and the time of forenoon were chosen, and a spacious field was selected so that people from all directions could easily attend. Furthermore, the public was heavily urged to gather, with announcements that the state magicians were assembling to protect the country from a grave threat. Thus, every citizen was encouraged to attend to support and cheer them on. Those familiar with Arabic literary tastes will recognize the tone of instigation and urgency in the question: 'Are you going to gather?' (Hal antum mujtami'oon). Some examples of this style have appeared earlier, and more will follow. The phrase 'Perhaps we will follow the magicians if they are the victors' contains the sentiment that the magicians were entering the field to protect their national pride, so everyone should hope for their victory and stand by them to boost their morale.

Pharaoh's encouragement of the magicians
When the magicians arrived before Pharaoh, they asked like typical professionals: 'Will we get a handsome reward if we turn out to be the victors?' Pharaoh reassured them, saying: 'Not only will you receive your reward, but you will also be granted a special favor of being included among the close companions of my court!'

Moses and the magicians in the arena
When Moses and the magicians faced each other in the arena, Moses allowed them to take the initiative, saying: 'Throw down whatever you wish to throw.' They threw down their ropes and staffs, which appeared to move, and swore: 'By the might of Pharaoh, we will surely be the victors!' In our view, the preposition 'bi' in 'bi-izzati Fir'awn' is used for taking an oath. Since Pharaoh was revered as a deity among them, they would swear by his honor and might before taking any major step. Testimonies of this practice are present in the Torah.

The staff of Moses shatters the illusion of sorcery
In response, Moses threw down his staff, and it began to swallow up all the illusions they had fabricated to deceive the people. The phrasing 'what they falsified' (ma ya'fikoon) suggests that the staff of Moses did not merely expose the trick performed by the magicians on that day, but rather invalidated their entire craft. Since their masterpiece was destroyed, it meant that the staff of Moses had shattered the very basis of their sorcery.

Verses 46-48
The magicians' acknowledgment of the truth
We have explained elsewhere that a magician is best suited to recognize the difference between mere sorcery and a true miracle, provided they retain a spark of honesty. Since these magicians possessed that sincerity, they fell prostrate the moment they witnessed the truth, declaring their faith before the entire gathering. They made it explicit: 'We believe in the Lord of the worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron!' This was to leave no room for ambiguity, specifically referencing the 'Lord of the worlds' whom Pharaoh had mocked in verses 16 and 24. This is a testament to the powerful effect of true faith!

Pharaoh's claim of a conspiracy
This defeat in a packed arena brought immense humiliation to Pharaoh and his court, but Pharaoh was not one to accept defeat easily. To control the situation, he immediately fabricated a conspiracy theory. He said to the magicians: 'Your declaration of faith in this man before everyone, and without my permission, proves that he is your leader who taught you magic. You had a pre-planned conspiracy to stage this defeat so that his influence would be established in the hearts of the people, paving the way for a revolution in the state. You have conspired against the kingdom, and I will punish you and your group with the punishment reserved for rebels. Your hands and feet will be cut off on alternate sides, and then you will be crucified as a warning to all.'

His words 'You believed in him before I gave you permission' are highly significant. He wanted to convey: 'If you had genuinely found this man to be truthful, your duty as loyal subjects was to bring this to my notice first so that I could make a decision. But by acting on your own, you have proven that this entire drama of victory and defeat was a conspiracy.'

Verses 50-51
'La dayr' means there is no harm or loss. 'An kunna' is originally 'Li-an kunna', with the preposition 'li' omitted according to standard Arabic usage.

The miracle of the triumph of faith
Pharaoh issued a severe threat, but he failed to realize that these magicians were no longer the professional artists who had humbly begged for rewards. A single glimpse of the light of faith had elevated them to a station where Pharaoh and his entire empire held no more value in their eyes than the wing of a gnat. They answered fearlessly: 'There is no harm in this punishment; we will ultimately return to our Lord. If you deem us guilty of conspiracy because we believed in Moses without your permission, we welcome this punishment. We hope our Lord will forgive our past mistakes because we were the first to believe.'

While this sudden transformation of the magicians to such a high level of spiritual realization may seem astonishing to ordinary people, it is not surprising. Lord Jesus also told his unlettered disciples: 'When they bring you before courts in my name, do not worry beforehand about what to say, for the Holy Spirit will put the words in your hearts at that very moment.'

The migration of Moses and their pursuit by Pharaoh
Some details of the narrative are omitted here. We have explained elsewhere, in the light of both the Qur'an and the Torah, that a long struggle ensued between Moses and Pharaoh after this contest. During this period, Egypt was struck by various plagues that brought the country to the brink of ruin. Distressed by these calamities, Pharaoh's courtiers advised him to let the Children of Israel go, warning him that the country would otherwise be destroyed. Pharaoh finally relented and permitted them to leave, but as soon as Moses set out with his people, Pharaoh changed his mind. He immediately sent messengers throughout the land to gather commanders, soldiers, and war materials for a pursuit. The verse points to this situation, stating: 'We directed Moses through revelation to set out with the Children of Israel by night, and warned him that they would be pursued despite the permission. You must remain vigilant against this danger.'

Verses 53-56
Pharaoh caught in his own trap
Pharaoh sent heralds to all cities to gather his commanders and soldiers. Since the Copts were highly intimidated by the deeds of Moses, Pharaoh used propaganda to boost their morale, saying: 'What is the standing of these Israelites! They are but a small band trying to challenge us! They provoke us with their actions, but we are a mighty host, fully alert and prepared! We will not allow them to cause any mischief in the land or escape our grasp!'

Verses 57-58
Meaning, Allah utilized His plan to make them leave their gardens, springs, treasures, and grand palaces, only to lead them to their drowning in the sea with all their military might.

An eloquent omission
After the word 'Kadhālik' (Thus), words like 'We dealt with the criminals' or similar expressions are omitted. This omission adds a powerful rhetorical force that is well appreciated by those of refined taste.

A specific style of using pronouns
In 'And We caused the Children of Israel to inherit them' (Wa awrathnāhā Bani Isra'il), the pronoun does not refer to the exact same gardens and palaces from which the Copts were expelled, but rather to blessings of the same kind that the Children of Israel received in the land of Palestine after leaving Egypt. This is explicitly stated in another place: 'And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern regions of the land and the western ones, which We had blessed' (Al-A'raf: 137). 'The blessed land' clearly refers to Palestine. It was this fertile region that came into the possession of the Children of Israel after their exodus and wilderness years. History does not support the view that the Children of Israel took possession of Egypt or its territories.

One might think that we have stretched the rules of grammar to identify the referent of the pronoun, and that the apparent meaning of the verse suggests they inherited the exact same gardens and treasures left by the Egyptians. However, this is not the case. In high Arabic literature, pronouns are used in this manner. We have cited several examples of this in this book. A clear example is found in Surah al-Ma'idah, where Muslims are cautioned against asking questions similar to those asked by the Children of Israel: 'A people before you asked such questions, and then became disbelievers because of them' (Al-Ma'idah: 101). Clearly, the pronoun does not refer to the exact same questions, but to questions of the same nature. Similarly, in the verse under discussion, the reference is not to the exact same gardens and treasures, but to blessings of the same kind received by the Children of Israel in Palestine after their migration.

Finally, the Egyptians pursued the Children of Israel at sunrise. When the Children of Israel saw the sea ahead and Pharaoh's army behind them, they panicked, crying out that they were trapped. The Torah mentions that they began to complain to Moses, asking if there were no graves in Egypt that he had brought them to die in the wilderness. Moses reassured them, saying: 'Do not fear. My Lord is with me; He will guide us to a way out. Though the sea is ahead and Pharaoh's army is behind, and the way appears blocked, my Lord's promise to me will surely be fulfilled. No harm will reach you; God will manifest His power and open a way for us.' The Torah describes this event as follows:

'When the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his officials changed their minds about them and said, "What have we done? We have let the Israelites go and have lost their services!" So he had his chariot made ready and took his army with him... The Egyptians—all Pharaoh’s horses and chariots, horsemen and troops—pursued the Israelites and overtook them... As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up, and there were the Egyptians, marching after them. They were terrified and cried out to the Lord. They said to Moses, "Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us to the desert to die?"... Moses answered the people, "Do not be afraid. Stand firm and you will see the deliverance the Lord will bring you today. The Egyptians you see today you will never see again. The Lord will fight for you; you need only to be still."' (Exodus 14:5-14)

Verses 63-66
This was a test of trust in Allah for Moses and his people. In this test, the Children of Israel failed in line with their typical behavior, but Moses succeeded completely. After passing this test, Allah manifested His power. Moses was commanded to strike the sea with his staff, and he complied. The Torah mentions that a strong wind blew, dividing the sea into two parts, forming a wall of water on each side and leaving a dry path in the middle. Moses led his people through this path. Allah's plan led the Egyptians to the same spot, and they pursued them into the sea with all their chariots and horsemen. Subsequently, by divine command, the wind that had held back the water ceased. Moses and his people safely reached the shore, while the sea collapsed back onto the Egyptians, drowning the entire army as they tried to flee. The Torah describes it thus:

'Then Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and all that night the Lord drove the sea back with a strong east wind and turned it into dry land. The waters were divided, and the Israelites went through the sea on dry ground, with a wall of water on their right and on their left. The Egyptians pursued them, and all Pharaoh’s horses and chariots and horsemen followed them into the sea... Then the Lord said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the sea so that the waters may flow back over the Egyptians and their chariots and horsemen." Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and at daybreak the sea went back to its place. The Egyptians were fleeing toward it, and the Lord swept them into the sea.' (Exodus 14:21-27)

The word 'tawd' used in the verse means a mountain or a high heap, which the Torah describes as a wall. Regarding the exact crossing point, the view of Ustadh-e-Imam Maulana Farahi differs from that of the Christian and Jewish scholars. He has presented his view in his commentary of Surah adh-Dhariyat. Since his detailed arguments are not before me, I remain undecided on this point. The commentary of Surah adh-Dhariyat is included in the published collections of Farahi's works for those who wish to study it.

Verses 67-68
Refrain
This is the same refrain repeated at the end of the narrative. It means that if these people are seeking a sign, there is indeed a great lesson in this story. However, those who do not use their reason persist in demanding signs, and even after witnessing the greatest of signs, the majority of them—like Pharaoh's people—fail to believe. Allah can seize such rebels whenever He wills, as He is the All-Mighty, but He grants them respite to repent and reform, for He is also the Merciful.

The upcoming subject... Verses 69-104
The call of Prophet Abraham and his people's response
Next is the mention of Abraham's (peace be upon him) call and the response of his people, concluding with the same refrain as above. This shows that the purpose of relating this story is the same as the previous one.

It is particularly noteworthy that while the previous Surah mentioned Noah immediately after Moses without discussing the intermediate prophets, this Surah fills that gap by relating the story of Abraham, who is the most illustrious prophet between Noah and Moses. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

Verses 69-71
Abraham's question was not to seek information, but to express disdain: 'What are these useless and baseless things that you worship!' His people answered with the pride and zeal of jahiliyyah: 'We worship idols, and we will remain devoted to them despite your opposition. No matter how hard you try, we will not abandon our gods.'

Verses 73-74
To expose the helplessness of these idols, Abraham asked: 'Do they hear you when you call upon them, or do they benefit or harm you? If not, what is the point of worshipping them!' The people replied: 'We do not know about these things. We only know that we found our forefathers doing this, so we will continue to do the same. We cannot abandon the way of our ancestors just because you say so.'

It should be noted that these verses depict the stage of Abraham's call after he had fully established proof against them through all rational, traditional, and natural arguments. The upcoming verses will show that he subsequently declared his dissociation from them and migrated. At this stage, the tone of a prophet's address changes, as does the nature of the people's response. The prophet's words represent a final attempt to shake the intellect and conscience of the people, while the people's response represents a final display of stubbornness and defiance. Both aspects are clear from this dialogue.

Verses 75-77
Abraham's declaration of dissociation
The phrasing 'Have you considered...' (A-fa-ra'aytum) carries a tone of surprise, contempt, and dissociation. It means: 'Look at these things that you and your forefathers have been worshipping and are proud of. They are completely baseless. Hear my final decision regarding them: they are all my enemies, and I disassociate myself from them. I worship none but the Lord of the worlds.'

'They are my enemies' implies that these idols are traps set by Satan, the eternal enemy of mankind, whose main purpose is to divert people from the straight path. This was Abraham's declaration of dissociation before his migration. In Surah as-Saffat, his words at migration are: 'Indeed, I am going to my Lord; He will guide me' (as-Saffat: 99). This migration is also mentioned in Surah al-Ankabut, verse 26. A prophet is always a well-wisher, friend, and compassionate advisor to his people, but when the environment itself turns hostile and expects him to compromise his faith, he treats their false ways as an enemy and migrates to his Lord. This is why Abraham did not say 'I am their enemy,' but rather 'they are my enemies.'

Verses 78-82
After exposing the helplessness of his people's idols, Abraham described the attributes of the true Lord who alone deserves worship and obedience.

'Who created me, and He guides me' (Alladhi khalaqani fa-huwa yahdeen) means that the One who created me and guides me is the only One worthy of my worship. This is both a statement of fact and a logical necessity, for creation and guidance are rationally linked. Allah did not merely create us; He endowed us with high faculties and taught us how to utilize them. He created us on a sound nature and gave us intellect to distinguish between truth and falsehood, good and evil, and to reach abstract realities from physical perceptions. Thus, He says: 'Who created and proportioned, and Who destined and guided' (al-A'la: 2-3).

Spiritual nourishment
This guidance is a necessary requirement of God's attribute of creation. It is inconsistent with His mercy and wisdom that He would create a human being with such elaborate design and then leave him to wander in darkness. To do so would make creation a purposeless exercise and an injustice, which is contrary to His glory. Therefore, just as He provided for man's material needs, He provided for his spiritual needs. Since man's ultimate perfection depends on this spiritual fulfillment, Allah did not leave it to human intellect alone, but arranged for prophethood and messengership. Abraham's message was that the One who is the source of all these favors deserves worship, not these stone idols that can neither hear, see, benefit, nor harm.

'And it is He who feeds me and gives me drink, and when I am ill, it is He who cures me' (Walladhi huwa yut'imuni wa yasqeen, wa idha maridtu fa-huwa yashfeen). After spiritual and intellectual rububiyyah, he drew attention to material nourishment: my Lord is the One who feeds me and gives me drink, and when I fall ill, He restores my health out of His mercy. This was also a blow to his people's superstitious belief that illness is caused by the anger of idols and cure is obtained by pleasing them. Abraham clarified that none of these idols have any role in these matters; rather, everything is in the hands of the Lord of the universe.

The phrasing 'And when I am ill, He cures me' is also noteworthy. He attributed the acts of feeding, drinking, and curing directly to Allah, but attributed illness to himself. This was out of deep respect for God, reflecting the reality that while all blessings come from His grace, any suffering or illness is often a result of one's own negligence. Though everything occurs by God's permission, human actions play a role in physical suffering, which is why Abraham attributed illness to himself, saying 'when I am ill' instead of 'when He makes me ill.'

Arguing for the Day of Judgment from creation and guidance
'And Who will cause me to die and then bring me to life' (Walladhi yumeetuni thumma yuhyeen). This points to the logical end of creation, guidance, and nourishment: the One who will cause me to die will surely bring me back to life. Since He created me the first time without any difficulty, doing so again is simple for Him, and it is a necessary requirement of His guidance and care. It is impossible that He would make such elaborate arrangements for my guidance and nourishment only for this life to end in nothingness.

'And Who I hope will forgive me my sin on the Day of Recompense' (Walladhi atma'u an yaghfira lee khatee'atee Yawmad-Deen). This refers to the Day of Judgment, which everyone must face. However, instead of simply stating its occurrence as a cold fact, Abraham expressed his hope in his Lord for that Day. The occurrence of that Day is an absolute certainty; therefore, one must prepare for it and hope for the mercy of the Lord, who will overlook the shortcomings of those who lived their lives in His fear.

A specific style of address based on the situation
It is worth noting that while Abraham presented his message with deep logical connection, he chose to express it as personal feelings and convictions rather than an abstract debate. This was because he was at the stage of dissociation and migration, where intellectual debates with his people were no longer useful. Therefore, he expressed his personal convictions in a moving manner, hoping it might touch their hearts. When a spark of life remains in a people, this style of address is highly effective. The Qur'an contains many such eloquent examples.

Verses 83-85
Abraham's prayer at the time of migration
This is the prayer Abraham (peace be upon him) made at the critical juncture of his dissociation and migration.

'My Lord, grant me authority' (Rabbi hab lee hukman). As explained under verse 21, 'Hukm' refers to the sound power of judgment and decision-making that is specially granted to prophets. Since Abraham was taking a major step and needed correct judgment and divine guidance in the upcoming phases, he prayed for this first.

'And join me with the righteous' (Wa alhiqnee bis-saliheen). Since he had dissociated himself from his family and nation for the sake of truth, he prayed that he be granted the company of the righteous instead of the wicked, both in this world and the hereafter.

Abraham's prayer for the spread of his message
'And grant me a reputation of honor among later generations' (Waj'al lee lisana sidqin fil-akhireen). Here, 'lisan' (tongue) means reputation or mention, and since it is coupled with 'sidq' (truth), it refers to an honorable and lasting mention.

Abraham prayed for the spread and stability of his message among future generations. The acceptance this prayer received is unparalleled in history. Every prophet who came after him among the Children of Israel carried his message, and finally, the Seal of Prophets, Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), made this call global and eternal.

Verses 86-87
Finally, Abraham (peace be upon him) prayed for Paradise for himself and forgiveness for his father.

'And do not disgrace me...' is a very moving plea for his father. The disgrace of one's close relatives, especially parents, is akin to one's own disgrace. Therefore, Abraham prayed that his father's punishment in Hell might not become a source of sorrow and disgrace for him on the Day of Judgment. Although Abraham fulfilled his duty of compassion toward his father through this prayer, Allah's law of justice is absolute. His prayer for his father was not accepted, and he was later stopped from praying for him once his father's persistent disbelief became clear.

Verses 88-89
Divine addition to Abraham's words
This is not part of Abraham's prayer, but a direct divine addition and explanation. It states that on that Day, neither wealth nor children will benefit anyone. Only those who present themselves before Allah with a sound heart (qalb saleem) will find acceptance. If anyone's children could have been of benefit, who was more fortunate than Azar, who had a son like Abraham? Yet, since even Abraham could not benefit his father, what can be said of others!

Meaning of 'qalb saleem'
A sound heart (qalb saleem) is a heart free from any taint of polytheism (shirk) and hypocrisy (nifaq). A heart that is divided or plagued by hypocrisy holds no value in the sight of Allah.

Verses 90-91
Honoring the righteous
'Izlaf' means to bring close. This refers to the honoring of the righteous: Paradise will be brought near to them. The reward for the good they did in this world will be kept ready and decorated for them. They will not have to travel to reach it; rather, it will be brought before them. Similarly, Hell will be made visible to the deviated. On the Day of Judgment, its veil will be lifted, and those who remained unmindful of it, thinking it was far off, will see that it was only waiting for the veil to be removed.

Verses 92-93
The end of the wicked
'Nasr' means to help others, and 'intisar' means to defend oneself. It will be said to those entering Hell: 'Where are those you used to worship besides Allah? Can they help you now, or even defend themselves?'

Verses 94-95
'Kubkiboo' means to be thrown headlong or cast down, carrying an intensive meaning. After this exposure, they, their idols, the leaders they followed, and the entire host of Iblees will be cast headlong into Hell.

Verses 96-98
Dispute between the leaders and their followers
'Ikhtisam' means to dispute with one another. As clarified in other places in the Qur'an, this dispute will take place between the leaders of disbelief and their followers. The followers will say: 'We followed you and ended up in this wretched state; will you bear a portion of our punishment?' The leaders will reply: 'We were deviated and we led you along; you are to blame for following us!' This dispute is briefly mentioned here: the followers will lament and swear to their leaders, saying: 'We were in clear error when we equated you with the Lord of the worlds.' Ibn Kathir explained 'when we equated you with the Lord of the worlds' as: 'We obeyed your commands just as the commands of the Lord of the worlds should have been obeyed.'

It should be noted that since the false deities worshipped by the polytheists—whether angels, jinn, or physical idols—were mere figments of their imagination, any actual dialogue with them is out of the question. On the Day of Judgment, it will become clear that these were just empty names. However, the leaders who initiated this worship, diverted people from God's service, and opposed the messengers will be present, and it is they whom the followers will address.

Verses 99-102
The followers' lament over their ruin
While the previous statement was addressed to their leaders, these words will be uttered by the followers out of deep regret, blaming themselves for their ruin: 'None but the wicked led us astray. Had they not deceived us, we would have been guided.' The context shows that they will refer to their leaders as 'the wicked.'

'So now we have no intercessors, nor a close friend' (Fa-ma lana min shafi'een, wa la sadeeqin hameem) are words of absolute despair. Those whose intercession they relied upon have vanished, and those whose flags they carried are nowhere to be seen to support them.

'If only we had another chance...' is their wish to return to the world to dissociate themselves from these leaders and join the believers. But this wish will remain unfulfilled, as the time for action has passed forever. These verses serve as a warning to those who blindly follow ancestral traditions and leaders, showing that such excuses will be of no benefit on the Day of Judgment; everyone must bear their own burden.

Verses 103-104
This is the same refrain explained above.

The upcoming subject... Verses 105-122
The call of Prophet Noah
Next is the story of Noah's (peace be upon him) call, his rejection by his people, the descent of punishment upon them, and finally, the same refrain. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

Rejecting one messenger is akin to rejecting all messengers
Although the nation rejected only one messenger—Noah—the plural term 'messengers' (mursaleen) is used. As explained elsewhere, the actual factor in acceptance or rejection is the mindset of the people. Since the call of all prophets has been the same, those who rejected Noah's call effectively rejected the call of every messenger. This highlights that the call of all prophets is consistent, the mindset of their opponents is identical, and their ultimate end is the same. This story is related here so that the opponents of the Prophet Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) may learn a lesson from it.

Verses 106-107
A trustworthy messenger
'Will you not fear?' (Ala yattaqun) is an invitation carrying a warning, and 'a trustworthy messenger' (rasulun ameen) is a response to their accusation that he was an impostor. It means: 'I am not a liar or a false claimant; I am a messenger of God, conveying to you with absolute honesty and trustworthiness exactly what I have been commanded to convey.'

فاتقوا اللہ واطیعون 108
If you seek safety, abandon this defiance and fear Allah's grasp. The way to do this is to listen to me, obey my counsel, and stop following the rebellious leaders who are leading you to ruin.

Verses 109-110
Encouraging reflection and expressing selflessness
This statement encourages the listeners to reflect on the truth and expresses complete selflessness: 'I am presenting this to you solely for your own well-being; I do not ask for any reward or payment that might make you avoid my words. If you listen and believe, you will benefit yourselves. I have no personal interest in it; my reward is with my Lord, and He will not deprive me of it. Therefore, fear the wrath of Allah and obey me!'

Excuses of a stubborn heart
In response to Noah's sincere and compassionate advice, the arrogant chiefs of his nation said: 'Only the lowest of our people have followed you; how can we, the nobility, sit alongside them in your assembly? If you want us to join you, distance yourself from them first.' This demand shows that they had no logical counter-argument against Noah's call, yet they refused to accept defeat and sought excuses in their pride, claiming they could not stand alongside poor and low-status people. A stubborn heart always finds an excuse!

Verses 112-115
Honor depends on one's actions
Noah gave an exceptionally eloquent response to their objection, saying: 'What knowledge do I have of what they used to do? Honor and status depend on a person's deeds and character, not on lineage, family, or wealth. Their past is for my Lord to judge; as for their present, I see that they have believed in their Lord and fear Him.'

'If you only realized' means: 'If you had any true understanding, you would see things as I do, but your minds are clouded by the notion that honor depends on wealth. Thus, their poverty has become a barrier to your acceptance of the truth.'

'And I am not one to drive away the believers; I am only a clear warner' implies: 'If your belief is conditional on my driving away these believers, I will never do that. If you refuse to believe because of them, the responsibility is yours. My duty is only to warn, and I have fulfilled it.'

The nation's final threat
Having lost the debate on rational grounds, Noah's nation issued their final threat: 'If you do not stop this call, O Noah, we will stone you to death.' The phrasing suggests they had threatened him with stoning before, but Noah had ignored it. Now, they presented their final decision: 'Do not treat our threat lightly; we will carry out what we say.'

Verses 117-118
Noah's prayer for victory
Following this final threat, and seeing no hope of their belief, Noah acted in accordance with the practice of messengers and prayed to his Lord: 'My Lord, my people have rejected me, so make a decisive judgment between me and them, and deliver me and the believers with me.' The emphasis on 'decisive judgment' (fathan) suggests he prayed for the manifestation of the divine law that brings destruction to the deniers and deliverance to the messenger and his companions.

Verses 119-120
An answered prayer
Noah's prayer was made at the right moment and was immediately answered. The threatened punishment came in the form of a flood that drowned the entire nation, while Noah and his companions were saved.

'In the laden ship' (fil-fulkil-mashhoon). 'Mashhoon' means filled or packed. This refers to the instruction given to Noah to take pairs of all creatures into the ark, as detailed in other places. The ark was built under divine guidance to save what was intended to be saved, while everything else was left to drown.

Verses 121-122
Refrain
This is the same refrain explained above. If they are seeking a sign, there is indeed a great sign in this historical account. But these are stubborn people who seek excuses, and most of them will not believe even after witnessing major signs. Your Lord can seize them whenever He wills, as He is the All-Mighty, but He grants them respite because He is also the Merciful.

The upcoming subject... Verses 123-140
It will be recalled that the previous Surah mentioned Moses and Noah and left out the intermediate nations with a brief reference. This Surah presents the detailed accounts of those intermediate nations, showing how they treated their messengers and what their ultimate end was. The order is historical, and each story concludes with the same refrain. First, the account of the nation of 'Ad is presented.

Verses 123-126
All messengers share the same creed; hence, rejecting one is rejecting all
The reason for using the plural 'messengers' (mursaleen) was explained under verse 105. Rejecting one messenger is indeed a rejection of all messengers. This is why the Qur'an does not accept the claim of faith made by the People of the Book when they make distinctions among God's messengers. Since the call and creed of all messengers are one, anyone who rejects one rejects them all.

'Their brother Hud' (Akhuhum Hud) indicates that Hud (peace be upon him) was not a stranger to them. They were fully aware of his life and character, and had no basis to accuse him of falsehood. Refusing to believe such a person is a clear sign of their own spiritual ruin.

Rebuking those who turn away
We have pointed out the aspects of encouragement and warning in this verse. It also carries a rebuke to those who refused to listen to Hud, implying: 'O people, at least listen to my words; I am not asking for any fee or reward that might burden you. My reward is with my Lord; I am offering this to you entirely free.'

129-128
'Ree' refers to high ground visible from a distance.
'Ayah' here is used in the sense of a sign or a monument.
'Masani' can refer to fortresses and strongholds, or to palaces and grand mansions.
In eloquent Arabic, 'La'allakum' can carry the meaning of 'as if'.

Warning against a corrupt civilization
Hud (peace be upon him) warned his people against their corrupt civilization, reminding them that buildings and houses are meant for utility, not for display, pride, and competition. He said: 'Your state is such that a passion for construction has possessed you; every wealthy person among you is busy building monuments on every high and scenic spot for display and entertainment, rather than for any real individual or collective need. You are behaving as if you are to remain in this world forever, treating a temporary stop as your permanent home and focusing all your attention on its development, unmindful of the true destination ahead.'

This was addressed to the chiefs and wealthy of his nation. When the afterlife is ignored and the world becomes the sole objective, the very spirit of civilization becomes corrupt. Those with resources indulge in endless construction, while the poor are left without even a basic shelter, often dying of extreme weather right outside these grand mansions.

This civilizational corruption stems from two main factors: prioritizing luxury over basic needs, and forgetting the afterlife. Hud pointed to both causes: the first through the word 'tab'athoon' (acting in vain) and the second through 'la'allakum takhludoon' (as if you are to live forever). This diagnosis fits our own modern civilization perfectly. We will discuss the aspects of sound and corrupt civilizations further in Surah an-Naml.

Warning against corruption on earth
The previous verse referred to their internal spiritual corruption, while this verse points to their external corruption on earth. War fought merely to display power and subjugate others is a form of corruption. It is justified only when fought to eliminate evil and establish justice, in which case it becomes a noble act of worship. The battles fought by the righteous have always been in self-defense or to eradicate tyranny for the sake of truth. Hud tried to stop his people from their aggressive ways, saying: 'Your battles are tyrannical; you shed blood on earth to enslave others, which is a grave form of corruption.'

Verses 131-135
'Fear Allah and obey me' (Fattaqulla-ha wa atee'oon) is a statement reported from almost every messenger in the Qur'an. It is a compassionate plea made at the final stage to warn the nation to fear Allah, abandon their wrong ways, and follow the messenger instead of their corrupt leaders.

Respite disguised as blessings (Istidraj)
'And fear Him who aided you with what you know' (Wattaqulladhee amaddakum bi-ma ta'lamoon). Hud warned his people by reminding them of Allah's blessings rather than His wrath alone. He implied: 'There is no need to list the blessings of wealth, children, gardens, and springs; you see them with your own eyes. The One who gave you these did not do so for you to ignore the afterlife and behave arrogantly on earth, but so that you may live as His grateful servants. Otherwise, a dreadful punishment is hovering over you.' This refers to the punishment that inevitably befell the nation upon their rejection of the messenger.

Those who possess true recognition of Allah fear His blessings more than His trials, lest they fail to show gratitude and the blessings become a means of gradual ruin (istidraj). But very few understand this secret.

The height of spiritual hardness
In response to Hud's sincere and compassionate warnings, his nation replied with contempt and mockery: 'It is all the same to us whether you warn us or not; we are not going to change our ways.' The phrasing 'whether you are of those who advise' carries a subtle sarcasm, implying: 'We hold no value for your anxiety regarding our future; we consider it a form of madness.'

Sometimes, even if a person cannot accept advice, they still appreciate the sincerity of the adviser. But it is the ultimate height of spiritual hardness when a person not only rejects the advice but also mocks the compassion of the adviser. This was the attitude of Hud's nation. When he pleaded with them to at least listen to him, they told him to keep his advice to himself, as they had no need for it.

Verses 137-138
'Khuluqul-awwaleen' refers to the ancient ways or traditions of the past.

They said: 'This punishment you are warning us of is nothing new; such warnings have been given in the past as well, but they were mere empty threats. No punishment came in the past, and none will come now. People like you have always engaged in such wishful thinking, and we are not going to be intimidated by these imaginary fears.' This theme is also mentioned in Surah al-Mu'minun, verse 83, and Surah an-Naml, verse 68: 'We have been promised this, we and our forefathers, before; this is nothing but legends of the former peoples.'

Those who are unmindful of the truth have always treated warnings of punishment and resurrection as mere fables, arguing that since the promised Day has not yet arrived, it will never arrive. Similarly, when historical accounts of past destructions are presented to them, they dismiss them as natural events, refusing to see any connection between a nation's moral conduct and its ultimate fate.

Verses 139-140
It is stated that they rejected Hud (peace be upon him), and consequently, Allah destroyed them. This is followed by the same refrain, offering consolation to the Prophet Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and a warning to his opponents. It implies that if they seek a sign, there is a great sign in this history. Those who seek excuses are granted respite for a specified time, but they cannot escape the grasp of the All-Mighty.

The upcoming subject... Verses 141-159
The call of Prophet Salih
Next is the story of the nation of Thamud and their prophet, Salih (peace be upon him), concluding with the same refrain. The consistency in the message of all prophets, the attitude of their nations, and their ultimate end is evident here as well. Since the Quraysh were displaying the exact same attitude toward their Prophet, why would their end be any different from that of their predecessors? Read the verses in this light.

Verses 141-144
Response to the nation's accusation and the warning against corrupt leaders
These verses are identical to those in the previous stories and have been explained there. Salih's declaration 'Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger' is a response to his nation's accusation that his claim was false and aimed at establishing dominance. He clarified: 'I am not an impostor; I am a messenger of God, conveying His message to you with absolute honesty.'

'Fear Allah and obey me' is a warning to the people not to be deceived by their corrupt leaders, urging them to fear Allah's grasp before the promised Day arrives.

Selflessness is a prerequisite for serving creation
The aspects of encouragement, warning, and selflessness in this verse have been explained earlier. Selflessness is a prerequisite for serving creation. While it is impossible to stop people from talking, the prophets openly declared that they sought no reward from the people. Though maintaining such selflessness is challenging, history shows that the message of truth has always been spread by those who sought no worldly return from creation.

Verses 146-149
Blessings without gratitude lead to gradual ruin (Istidraj)
The previous verse served as an introduction. Here, Salih warned his people of the real danger that was looming over them, which they, in their worldly pride, failed to see. They mistook their material prosperity for permanent strength, but Salih warned them that this prosperity without gratitude was a sign of impending doom. As noted in the story of Hud, blessings without gratitude lead to gradual ruin (istidraj).

Blessings are a trust from the Provider
The phrasing 'in what is here' (fee ma hahuna) points to their luxurious lifestyle, which is further detailed in the subsequent words. If prosperity leads to negligence, it becomes a trial. Every blessing demands gratitude, which requires the recipient to remain vigilant lest they fail to fulfill its rights. A blessing is not an inherent right, but a trust; therefore, those with more resources should be more vigilant. A person who receives blessings and lives carelessly, thinking it is their right, is foolish. This carelessness, described here as 'secure' (amineen), leads to the same fate as a silkworm that spins silk around itself until it dies within its own creation. The Qur'an's arguments from God's rububiyyah to the Resurrection emphasize that every blessing carries responsibility—a reality acknowledged by sound human nature and intellect.

'Its spadix is gentle' (tal'uha hadeem) refers to dates whose clusters are tightly packed. The detailed description of gardens and date palms serves to elaborate on 'in what is here'. In ancient Arabia, a prime garden was one with grapes, pomegranates, running springs, crop fields, and a border of date palms. Salih asked his people: 'Do you think the true Owner of these gardens has left you to enjoy them forever without any accountability?' This same reality is frequently illustrated through parables in the Gospels.

'And you carve houses from the mountains, insolently' (Wa tanhitoona minal-jibali buyootan fariheen). 'Farah' means to be jubilant, proud, or boastful. This attitude is a natural result of carelessness and a disregard for the future, which is why Surah al-Hijr, verse 82, describes this construction as a search for security.

History shows that the Thamud made great advancements in stone carving. Their wealthy carved grand palaces out of mountains for display and pride, much like the 'Ad. This focus on luxury and pride at the expense of social welfare leads to a short-lived civilization that quickly turns into ruins. Salih warned his people that they would not be left to enjoy these creations forever, as their respite was coming to an end.

Verses 150-152
Salih repeated the warning mentioned in verse 144, urging the people to fear Allah and obey him while there was still time. He explicitly told them: 'Do not follow the commands of those who transgress the limits, who cause corruption on earth.' Though these leaders claimed to be reformers, they were actually causing corruption. We have explained in Surah al-Baqarah that just as the stability of the universe depends on a single divine will, the reform of the earth depends on the implementation of His law. Any other system or law introduced on earth disrupts its peace and justice, regardless of how appealing its name may be.

Verses 153-154
The nation's response to Salih
'Musahhar' carries an intensive meaning compared to 'mas'hoor' (bewitched). The nation responded to Salih's warnings, saying: 'You appear to be completely bewitched; some spell has affected your mind, which is why you see dangers in our progress and try to intimidate us. What makes you think we should accept you as a messenger of God and abandon our progress? You are just a human being like us. If God wanted to send a messenger, why did He not send an angel or a supernatural being? We believe you are a liar. If you are indeed truthful, bring us a sign to prove that you are a messenger and that the punishment you warn of is real.'

Verses 155-156
Determining the sign of punishment
Following this decisive demand, Salih (peace be upon him) designated a she-camel—which may have belonged to him—as the sign of punishment. He warned them: 'This she-camel is a test for you. If you harm her, the punishment of Allah will descend upon you. Do not cause her any harm!' He also set a condition for the water resource: 'She will have her turn to drink on one day, and your livestock will have their turn on the next day.' Since there was only one watering place, this condition made the test challenging. Salih wanted to see if they would respect this restriction, for if they dared to harm the she-camel, it would prove they were ready to harm the messenger himself. When a nation reaches this level of rebellion, the divine punishment inevitably descends.

نہ مروھا فاصبحوا ندمین 157
The foolishness of one can bring punishment upon all
These wicked chiefs could not tolerate a restriction where Salih's she-camel drank on her designated day. Therefore, they hamstrung her. Although the act was committed by a single individual, the Qur'an attributes it to the entire nation because the rest were pleased with the act and did not oppose it. Consequently, the punishment came upon them all.

'And they became regretful' (fa-asbahoo nadimeen) describes their state when they saw the punishment. It does not mean they repented out of sincerity. Other places in the Qur'an indicate they were granted a three-day respite, during which they could have repented to avert the punishment, but they failed to utilize this opportunity.

Verses 158-159
This is the same refrain explained above, and its placement here is highly appropriate.

The upcoming subject... Verses 160-175
The story of Prophet Lot and his people
Next is the story of Lot (peace be upon him) and his people. While Hud and Salih warned their nations against civilizational corruption, Lot warned his people against a severe moral corruption. It should be noted that such moral evils do not exist in isolation, but arise from a disregard for Allah and the afterlife, affecting every aspect of life. However, based on the specific temperament of a nation, one particular evil may become dominant, which is what the Qur'an focuses on here. Lot warned his people of the consequences of their actions, but they ignored him and met the same fate as the other nations. The story concludes with the same refrain.

Verses 160-164
These verses are identical to those in the previous stories and have been explained there.

166-165
The deviation of Lot's people
This points to their gravest sin. We have explained elsewhere that homosexual behavior had become a widespread fashion among Lot's people. When such moral degradation becomes socially accepted, a nation becomes a source of impurity on earth, and Allah purifies His earth by destroying them.

The words 'of the worlds' (minal-alameen) highlight their spiritual blindness: 'Of all creation, do you approach males, ignoring the natural order established by your Lord?' Surah an-Naml, verse 54, refers to this blindness: 'Do you commit this obscenity while you see?'

The consequences of reversing the natural order
'And leave what your Lord has created for you of your wives?' (Wa tadharoona ma khalaqa lakum Rabbukum min azwajikum). This explains their corrupt desires: once a sound nature is distorted, natural attraction toward the opposite sex dies out. Such people ignore the fertile ground and waste their resources, leading to their ultimate ruin.

'Rather, you are a transgressing people' (Bal antum qawmun 'adoon) is an expression of deep sorrow, implying that their actions went beyond the limits of human nature.

The nation's final threat
The nation responded to Lot's sincere advice with a decisive threat: 'If you do not stop, O Lot, you will surely be expelled.' The tone shows this was their final decision, making good on the threats they had been making previously.

قال انی عملکم من القالین 168
'Qala' means intense hatred and disgust. Lot gave a firm response to their threat, saying: 'Indeed, I am of those who detest your deed.' He made it clear that their threats would not stop him or make him compromise his stance: 'I detest this action, so do whatever you wish; I do not care.'

Using the plural form 'of those who detest' (minal-qaleen) adds force to his words, implying: 'I am not alone in detesting this; anyone with a sound nature and intellect would detest it, and I am among them. I will never compromise with your actions.'

Lot's prayer
According to Arabic grammar, some words are omitted in 'from what they do' (mimma ya'maloon), meaning: 'from the punishment of what they do.'

Following their threat, Lot realized that the final hour of judgment had arrived. Since they had decided to expel him, the divine punishment was imminent. He prayed for his family and companions: 'My Lord, deliver me and my family from the consequences of what they do.' As discussed elsewhere, 'family' (ahl) includes his followers; thus, any believers among his people were included in this prayer.

Verses 170-173
Subsequently, the punishment descended upon Lot's people, as detailed in Surah al-Hijr.

'An old woman' refers to Lot's wife, who sided with her people despite being part of Lot's household, and was thus destroyed with them. The term 'old woman' (ajooz) is used here in a diminishing sense.

Warning to the Quraysh
'And quite evil was the rain of those who were warned' (Fa-sa'a matarul-mundhareen) serves as a warning to the Quraysh that those who ignore the warnings of a messenger eventually face the rain of divine wrath.

Verses 174-175
This is the same refrain explained above.

The upcoming subject... Verses 176-191
The story of Prophet Shu'ayb and his people
Next is the story of Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) and his people, the dwellers of Madyan. Just as Lot warned his people against moral corruption, Shu'ayb warned his people against financial and economic corruption. But they treated him with the same defiance as the previous nations and met the same end. The story concludes with the same refrain.

'Dwellers of the wood' (Ashabul-Aykah) refers to the people of Madyan, who lived near a forest or thicket. Some commentators consider them as two distinct nations, but in our view, they are the same people.
These verses are identical to those in the previous stories and have been explained there.

Verses 181-182
Cheating in weights is contrary to the cosmic order
The people of Madyan were successful merchants, but in their neglect of Allah and the afterlife, they fell into the practice of cheating in weights and measures. This economic corruption is a social disease; once it spreads, it affects every merchant, leading to fraud, adulteration, and black-marketing.

Allah has created this universe with a balance (meezan), on which the heavens and the earth stand. If this balance is disrupted, the entire system collapses. This cosmic order serves as a guide for humans to maintain fairness and justice in all aspects of life, particularly in their economic dealings. We will discuss this further in Surah ar-Rahman.

Adulteration of goods
Meaning, do not deprive people of their rights by cheating in measures. A severe form of this is the adulteration of goods—such as mixing fat in ghee, sand in sugar, or water in milk. Through such deceit, a seller may appear to give the correct weight, but the actual quality of the product is compromised, often posing a hazard to health.

Ill-gotten wealth does not bring true growth
'And do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption' (Wa la ta'thaw fil-ardi mufsideen) means that economic growth achieved by disrupting justice is not blessed. Progress should not be sought through corruption, for wealth acquired through unlawful means does not lead to true prosperity; its ultimate end is ruin.

God's law of justice is the same for all generations
'Jibillah' means creation or generations. Shu'ayb drew attention to historical lessons: 'The Creator who created you is the same One who created the past generations. If He destroyed them for their corruption, His law will not change for you. If you had a different god, you might expect a different law, but since God is one, He will judge everyone with the same scale of justice. Therefore, fear Him and reform your ways.'

Verses 185-187
The deniers' demand for punishment
The nation responded to Shu'ayb with the same arguments used by other deniers: first, they accused him of being bewitched; second, they dismissed him as a mere human like themselves; and third, they challenged him to bring down a portion of the sky upon them if he was indeed truthful. Shu'ayb had warned them of the punishments that befell 'Ad, Thamud, and Lot's people, and they responded with this defiant demand.

قال ربی اعلم بما تعملون 188
Shu'ayb's complete trust in Allah
This is an expression of complete trust in Allah, implying: 'Bringing down a portion of the sky is not in my power; it is in Allah's hands. He is fully aware of your deeds, and His judgment will manifest at the exact time determined by His wisdom.'

We have explained the nature of the punishment that befell Shu'ayb's people under Surah al-A'raf, verse 91. In Surah Hud, verse 94, this punishment is described as a 'shriek' (sayhah), and here it is called the 'punishment of the Day of the Shadow' (adhabu Yawmiz-Zullah), indicating that it appeared as a dark cloud or shadow in the sky before engulfing them. This refers to a volcanic eruption or a rain of stones, combining earthquakes, winds, and dark clouds.
This is the same refrain repeated at the end of each story.

The upcoming subject... Verses 192-227
Conclusion of the Surah
Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, reconnecting with its introduction. The Surah began by consoling the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), stating that the Qur'an's divine origin is clear, and that those demanding signs are merely seeking excuses. If Allah willed, He could send a sign that would force them to submit, but He wants them to use their intellect. If they persist in their defiance, they will face the consequences predicted by the Qur'an. This was followed by the stories of the prophets to show that if the Quraysh adopt the same attitude, they will face the same end. The historical accounts concluded with Shu'ayb, and since the story of Moses was detailed earlier, the text returns to the central theme: the Qur'an is a revelation from Allah, not a product of devils or poetry. Its predictions exist in the previous scriptures, and the scholars of the Children of Israel are aware of them. If the disbelievers reject it, the Prophet should focus on his believing companions, while the wrongdoers will soon see their end. Read the verses in this light.

Verses 192-194
The status and origin of the Qur'an
The pronoun in 'Indeed, it' (Innahu) refers to the Qur'an. Since the context is clear, the pronoun is used without a direct noun.

'A revelation' (Tanzil) highlights that this is a gradual, carefully sent down message from the Lord of the worlds, not a product of devils or fortune-tellers. Therefore, respect its value and do not invite ruin by rejecting it.

'The Trustworthy Spirit' (Ar-Roohul-Ameen) is the title of Gabriel (peace be upon him). The attribute 'Trustworthy' (Ameen) highlights that Gabriel conveyed the message to the Prophet exactly as it was revealed, without any alteration. This refutes the false claims made by some opponents.

'Upon your heart, that you may be of the warners' (Ala qalbika li-takoona minal-mundhareen). The message was revealed to the Prophet's heart, the noblest part of his being, leaving no room for any satanic whisper. Though addressed to the Prophet, it serves to clarify to the opponents that this call originates from a pure source, was conveyed through a pure medium, and rests in a pure vessel.

'That you may be of the warners' defines the purpose of this revelation: to warn those who are unmindful of the afterlife about the consequences of their neglect. This Book is a warning, and the Prophet is a warner; therefore, people should listen to it attentively.

بلسان عربی مبین 95
Warning to the Arabs
This is a favor of Allah upon the Arabs: He revealed this Book in a clear Arabic language, which is both an honor for them and a source of complete proof. They cannot claim they were unable to understand it. If they reject it after this clarity, the responsibility lies entirely on them.

Verses 196-197
Testimony of previous scriptures in favor of the Qur'an
This points to the testimony of previous scriptures, showing that the predictions of the Qur'an and its Prophet exist in the scrolls of the earlier prophets. We have referenced these predictions in their respective places. The scholars of the Children of Israel were well aware of them, and while some concealed them, the sincere among them acknowledged the truth and believed. The Qur'an praises their sincerity in various places.

Referring to these predictions and scholars, the text asks the opponents: 'Is this testimony not a sufficient sign for them, that they demand further physical signs?' Although the Arabs respected the religious knowledge of the People of the Book, their hostility toward the Prophet led them to accuse those who supported him of conspiring with him.

Verses 198-199
