This further explains the favor mentioned in verse 195: 'And if We had revealed it to a non-Arab in a non-Arabic language, they would have excused themselves, saying they could not understand it.' But now they have no excuse, as it has been revealed in their own eloquent language and through a noble person from among themselves. Surah Fussilat, verse 44, presents this theme: 'And if We had made it a non-Arabic Qur'an, they would have said: "Why are its verses not explained in detail? Is it a foreign Book and an Arab messenger?"' Surah an-Nahl, verse 103, also discusses an aspect of this.

Verses 200-203
'Salaka' means to insert or inject something into another. Here it is used in this sense.

The real ailment of the opponents
This consoles the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), explaining that their aversion is not due to any defect in the Qur'an, but due to the corruption of their own hearts. Their persistent sinful lives have hardened their hearts, depriving them of the capacity to accept the truth. Like a corrupted stomach that rejects wholesome food, their hearts reject the upright message of the Qur'an. This is in accordance with the divine law regarding those who distort their own nature. They will only believe when they witness the promised punishment, which will overtake them suddenly, leaving them to cry out in regret, begging for respite when it is too late.

Verses 204-207
The question is rhetorical, expressing surprise: 'Do they seek to hasten Our punishment? Even if We grant them years of enjoyment, it will not avert what is promised.' The respite granted to them is according to divine wisdom, and if they mistake this delay for security and mock the warning, it only adds to their ultimate ruin.

Verses 208-209
Warning to the Quraysh
This reminds them of the divine law: Allah, being Just and Merciful, never destroys a nation without first sending a warner to establish proof. 'As a reminder' (Dhikra) highlights that destroying a nation without warning would be unjust, and Allah is never unjust. This warnings the Quraysh that a warner has arrived; if they reject him, their destruction will be a matter of absolute justice.

And the devils have not brought it down
This presents the negative aspect of the statement in verse 192. Combining both aspects, the meaning is: this is a revelation from Allah, not a product of devils as the Quraysh claim.

This provides arguments showing why this cannot be a satanic message.

First: 'And it is not suited for them' (Wa ma yanbaghee lahum), meaning such a message is contrary to the mission and nature of devils. Why would they reveal a message that destroys their own authority? Lord Jesus gave a similar response to the Pharisees when they accused him of casting out demons by the power of Beelzebub: 'Every kingdom divided against itself will be ruined, and a house divided against itself will fall. If Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand?' (Luke 11:17-18). The Qur'an expresses this in the words: 'And it is not suited for them.'

Second: 'Nor are they able' (Wa ma yastatee'oon). They cannot produce such a message. The Qur'an challenged the Quraysh to produce a single Surah like it with the help of their poets and jinn, but they failed, proving that this is beyond the capacity of devils.

Third: 'Indeed, they are from hearing removed' (Innahum 'anis-sam'i la-ma'zuloon). They have no access to the celestial realm. If you think they overhear secrets, that is a mere illusion; they have been completely blocked from doing so. Surah al-Jinn, verses 8-9, describes this: 'And we have sought to reach the heaven but found it filled with powerful guards and burning flames. And we used to sit therein in positions for hearing, but whoever listens now will find a burning flame waiting for him.' This points to a celestial arrangement protecting the Qur'an from any satanic interference, which we will discuss further in Surah al-Jinn.

Verses of transition
These verses are addressed to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but the message is directed at the deniers. It warns: 'Since the truth has been made clear, do not associate any other deity with Allah, and remain firm on your stance despite their opposition, so that you may be saved from the promised punishment.'

Command to warn close relatives

Meaning: 'Warn your close relatives of this impending punishment. They are your kinsmen, and thus deserve your sincere counsel first.' It should be noted that the Prophet's close clan held the custodianship of the Kaaba and the leadership of the Arabs; warning them first was important for both familial and social reasons. If they accepted reform, the task of reforming the wider society would become much easier. This has been the practice of all prophets—addressing the leaders first. Lord Jesus also focused his initial efforts on the scholars of the Jews, and turned to the general public only after the leaders rejected his call. This command served to fulfill the rights of kinship and clear the path for the call.

And lower your wing to those who follow you of the believers
Regarding those who had already believed, the Prophet was commanded: 'Just as a hen lowers her wings to protect her chicks, treat these believers with compassion and care, so that none of them may face the promised punishment through negligence.' It should be noted that very few of his close relatives had believed at that stage; the early believers were mostly poor and weak members of society whom the Quraysh held in contempt. Yet, the Prophet was commanded to treat them with compassion and protect them.

Meaning: 'If your close relatives disobey you, declare your dissociation from them, saying: "Indeed, I am disassociated from what you do."' This declaration of dissociation is the final stage of warning, after which the migration of the messenger and the descent of punishment take place. This declaration is reported from all prophets, including Abraham's declaration to his father.

Meaning: 'Do not worry about the consequences of this declaration, but place your trust in the All-Mighty, the Merciful, who will assist you in all upcoming phases.' He is the All-Mighty, so His will cannot be opposed, and He is the Merciful, so He will bless those who leave their kinsmen for His sake. This transition is highly challenging for a prophet and his companions, as they cut ties with their society for an unknown future. Only those who have firm trust in the All-Mighty, the Merciful, can take such a step.

Verses 218-220
This is a beautiful commendation of the nightly prayers and devotion of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his Companions: 'Place your trust in your Lord, who sees you when you stand for prayer at night, seeking His pleasure. Your devotion is in His knowledge, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.' This reassurance implies that their efforts will not go to waste, and that the All-Mighty, the Merciful, will assist them in every difficulty.

There are various views regarding 'when you stand' (heena taqoom), but in light of 'and your movement among those who prostrate' (wa taqallubaka fis-sajideen), the view referring to the night prayer (Tahajjud) is the most robust. We have explained that as the challenges of the call increased, the Prophet's nightly prayers and devotion also increased under divine instruction. This will be further detailed in Surah at-Toor and Surah Qaf.

'Taqallub' means movement or going to and fro.

Commendation of the Prophet's and Companions' night prayers
Just as the Prophet observed the night prayers himself, he encouraged his Companions to do the same, and would occasionally walk among them to see them engaged in worship. Lowering his wing to the believers also involved ensuring they remained spiritually alert. The Gospels describe that during his final trial, Lord Jesus retired to a hill to pray and urged his disciples to remain awake and pray so they would not fall into trial, checking on them repeatedly. 

'Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing' (Innahu Huwas-Samee'ul-Aleem). While trust was coupled with the attributes of Mighty and Merciful, the devotion is coupled with Hearing and Knowing, which is highly appropriate, as trust rests on these attributes.

Verses 221-223
Upon whom do the devils descend?
The preceding verses detailed the divine origin of the Qur'an, its protected medium of revelation, and its pure recipient, refuting the false claims of the opponents who accused the Prophet of being a fortune-teller under the influence of devils. Now, the text addresses these accusers directly: 'If you want to know upon whom the devils actually descend, let me tell you. They descend upon every sinful liar who listens to them, and most of them are liars.'

This is a description of the fortune-tellers (kahin) of Arabia who deceived the people. Their first attribute is 'affak' (sinful liar), as they fabricated lies to claim they received secrets from the unseen. Their second attribute is 'atheem' (sinful), as they were morally corrupt. Finally, their practice of deception is described: they would sit in meditation, pretending to listen to some unseen voice, and then present a rhymed, ambiguous phrase as revelation. This meditation is described as 'listening' (alqaws-sam'). This practice has been common among polytheistic cultures—fortune-tellers, priests, and sorcerers pretending to contact the unseen to deceive the simple-minded.

False claimants of fortune-telling
'And most of them are liars' (wa aktharuhum kadhiboon) means that while some fortune-tellers knew basic sorcery and contacted some jinn who told them some truths mixed with lies, most of them were complete frauds who simply ran this practice to make money by deceiving the public.

Verses 224-227
Refuting the accusation of poetry against the Prophet
After refuting the accusation of fortune-telling, these verses address the accusation of poetry. When the Quraysh called the Prophet a poet or the Qur'an poetry, they did not mean it in the literal sense, as the Qur'an does not follow the poetic meters of Arabia. Rather, seeing the profound effect of the Qur'an's eloquence on the hearts of the people, they tried to dismiss it by saying: 'This eloquence is similar to that of our great poets; treat him as a poet and do not give this Book the status of divine revelation.' They also believed that every great poet was accompanied by a jinn who inspired his poetry, and tried to suggest that the Prophet's revelation was also from a jinn rather than an angel.

In response, the Qur'an presents three criteria to distinguish between poetry and divine revelation.

First criterion: A tree is known by its fruit
'And the poets - the deviators follow them' (Wash-shu'ara'u yattabi'uhumul-ghawoon). A tree is known by its fruit, and a person by his companions. Look at the companions of the poets: they are the youth, the lovers of luxury, and the troublemakers. In contrast, look at the companions of the Prophet: they are the noble, the righteous, and the God-fearing. Poetry held great influence in Arabian society; poets could spark wars, such as the famous war of Bakr and Taghlib that lasted for decades. The moral state of their poetry can be seen from their greatest poet, Imru' al-Qays, whose work was filled with moral decadence, making him known as the 'deviated king' despite his poetic genius. Such poets naturally attracted companions of a similar nature.

Second criterion: Poets wander in every valley without a destination
'Do you not see that they wander in every valley?' (Alam tara annahum fee kulli wadin yaheemoon). Poets have no fixed destination or objective; they express whatever passing feeling they experience in a moving style, regardless of whether it leads to good or evil. In one verse they appear righteous, and in the next, sinful. Since human desire is inclined toward evil, the negative aspects of their poetry leave a lasting impression on the hearts of the people, while any good is lost in their inconsistent thoughts.

In contrast, the Qur'an has a consistent, fixed objective, and every verse leads to that same goal. No one can find any inconsistency in it, and its truth is supported by solid arguments that only a stubborn person would deny. Therefore, it makes no sense to call such a consistent message poetry.

Third criterion: Poets say what they do not do
'And that they say what they do not do' (Wa annahum yaqooloona ma la yaf'aloon). Poets are champions of words, not actions. In their poetry, they present themselves as heroes of war or ultimate lovers, but this is mere boasting; in practical life, they are inactive, living in their imaginary world. They praise virtues they never practice and boast of desires they never fulfill.

In contrast, the Prophet is the ultimate practical model of the message he presents. He is the first to practice the piety, devotion, and sacrifice he calls others to, and even his enemies could never point to any inconsistency between his actions and the Book he recited. Therefore, it is unjust to equate such a noble person with poets whose words do not match their deeds.

Verse 227
Exception for the believing poets
This is an exception to the general rule regarding poets, referring to the poets of the Prophet's court: 'Except those who believe and do righteous deeds.' Their faith has given their poetry a noble objective; they do not write out of desire, but to defend the truth and remember Allah. 'And remember Allah much' means their poetry is filled with the remembrance of Allah rather than pride and lust. 'And defend themselves after they were wronged' means they use their poetry only to counter the false accusations made against them and the believers, which is a right granted to every oppressed person.

'And those who have wronged are going to know to what kind of return they will be returned' (Wa sa-ya'lamulladheena zalamoo ayya munqalabin yanqaliboon). While the oppressed defend themselves with words now, the wrongdoers will soon witness their ultimate end.

Our modern poets and their poetry
Although this critique was directed at the poets of jahiliyyah, it applies to modern poets as well. If we evaluate their work against these criteria, very few will stand the test; most of them fall under 'they wander in every valley' and 'say what they do not do,' and their admirers are equally unmindful.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. What is correct is by the grace of Allah, and any error is due to my own shortcomings. May Allah protect us all from error, and praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
